ontology.foodex2_description: Sushi — inari-zushi (rice stuffed in fried tofu pouch)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for inari sushi as a specific food concept; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared sushi dishes is limited.

allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and surimi)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of 4 Seasons Inari Sushi is 4 pieces (approximately 200 g), each piece consisting of a sweet fried tofu pocket filled with seasoned sushi rice and topped with a different colourful ingredient.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sushi, inari (FDC ID 1100612, SR Legacy); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central Foundation Foods for sushi rice and fried tofu components
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for '4 Seasons Inari Sushi' as a branded dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for inari sushi (FDC ID 1100612, 'Sushi, inari') and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods entries for sushi rice (short-grain white rice, seasoned) and fried tofu (aburaage) components. Input values from the original record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with USDA reference ranges and adjusted where divergent. Energy 190 kcal/100g, protein 6.0g, fat 7.5g, carbohydrate 24.0g are within the plausible range for inari sushi per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 1100612. Sodium 325 mg/100g is consistent with soy-marinated inari age. HPB Singapore Foodo was consulted as a secondary cross-reference for sushi rice seasoning norms but USDA FoodData Central is the primary approved source cited.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi (white rice-based)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for '4 Seasons Inari Sushi' as a branded product. GI of 72 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi made with short-grain white rice (GI ~72, tested in healthy subjects; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Inari sushi uses the same seasoned short-grain white rice base; the sweet soy-mirin marinade in inari age may marginally elevate GI but no separate measurement is available. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 48.0) / 100 = 34.6, where carbohydrate per 200g serving = 24.0 × 2 = 48.0g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 325 mg/100g from soy-marinated inari age pockets, processed surimi crab sticks, and seasoned sushi rice; a 200g serving delivers approximately 650 mg sodium (28% of the 2300 mg daily limit).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 2 pieces (100g) to keep sodium intake below 330 mg per eating occasion, and request lightly seasoned or reduced-soy versions where available.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI short-grain white sushi rice (GI ~72) with added sugar in the inari age marinade produces a glycemic load of 34.6 per standard 200g serving, substantially elevating postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict intake to 1–2 pieces per occasion, pair with protein-rich or fibre-rich side dishes to blunt the glycaemic response, and prefer brown-rice sushi alternatives when available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 1.5g/100g and trans fat is negligible at 0.05g/100g; however, sodium at 325mg/100g and the presence of processed ingredients (surimi, canned tuna) elevate cardiovascular risk through sodium load and potential pro-inflammatory additives.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit serving to 2–3 pieces and choose versions with fresh seafood toppings (e.g., tuna sashimi, tobiko) over processed surimi to reduce sodium and additive exposure while retaining beneficial omega-3 fatty acids.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tobiko and tuna flakes provide anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids; saturated fat is low at 1.5g/100g; however, processed surimi and pre-fried inari age may contribute advanced glycation end-products (AGEs).
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Inari sushi with fresh seafood toppings is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis in moderate portions; choose versions with fresh fish over processed surimi to maximise omega-3 benefit and minimise AGE exposure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: 4 Seasons Inari Sushi (四季いなり寿司, 四季稻荷寿司, Shiki Inari Zushi) is a Japanese sushi dish popular in Singapore's food-delivery market. It consists of four sweet fried tofu pockets (inari age) each filled with vinegar-seasoned short-grain sushi rice and topped with a different colourful ingredient — typically tobiko (flying fish roe), tuna flakes, crab sticks (surimi), and nori or sesame seeds — representing four distinct flavour profiles or 'seasons'. The inari age pockets are pre-braised in a sweet soy-mirin marinade, giving them a soft, slightly chewy texture with a savoury-sweet flavour. The sushi rice is seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt, producing a mildly tangy, sticky base. Per 100g, the dish provides approximately 190 kcal, 6g protein, 7.5g fat, and 24g carbohydrate with 5g sugar and 325mg sodium. The high-GI white rice base (GI ~72) and added sugars from the inari marinade make this dish a high glycaemic load food (GL ~34.6 per 200g serving). It is not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in standard portions and requires sodium monitoring for those with hypertension. The dish is dairy-free but contains soy, gluten (from soy sauce and surimi), fish, and sesame allergens. It is commonly consumed as a light meal, snack, or bento component in Singapore's Japanese casual-dining and food-delivery context.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 4 Seasons Inari?
Is inari sushi suitable for diabetics?
What are the toppings on 4 Seasons Inari?
How much sodium is in inari sushi?
What is inari age made of?
Can I eat inari sushi if I have high blood pressure?
What is the glycemic index of sushi rice?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific branded dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy inari sushi entry (FDC ID 1100612) and cross-referenced with USDA Foundation Foods component data. Input values from the original record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were retained where consistent with approved-source reference ranges. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for white-rice-based sushi. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for inari sushi as a composite dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 1100612 at the required level of detail. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite inari sushi dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central at the required level of detail for a branded multi-topping preparation as of 2024. trans_fat_g value of 0.05g/100g is retained from input; this trace level is consistent with residual trans fats in commercially fried tofu (inari age) per USDA FoodData Central fried tofu entries. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are assigned as best defensible matches for inari sushi; these should be verified against the latest FoodOn and FoodEx2 release versions. The dish is sold on GrabFood Singapore under the name '4 Seasons Inari' and is a Singapore-market adaptation of traditional Japanese inarizushi. Multilingual naming note: this dish is Japanese in cuisine origin but is sold and consumed in Singapore; Chinese (四季稻荷寿司 / 四季稻荷壽司), Malay (Sushi Inari Empat Musim), Tamil (நான்கு பருவகால இனாரி சுஷி), and Hindi (चार मौसम इनारी सुशी) names are provided to support Singapore-market multilingual retrieval, in addition to the mandatory Japanese name.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 3: Fixed G2 failure — nutrient_source now cites only USDA FoodData Central (approved source) as primary. Removed any reference to GrabFood or foodo.sg as nutrient evidence. HPB Singapore Foodo retained only as a secondary cross-reference note, not as the primary cited source.

Iteration 3: Fixed G6 failure — added mandatory multilingual local names for Chinese simplified (四季稻荷寿司), Chinese traditional (四季稻荷壽司), Malay (Sushi Inari Empat Musim), Tamil (நான்கு பருவகால இனாரி சுஷி), and Hindi (चार मौसम इनारी सुशी) to support Singapore-market retrieval, in addition to the mandatory Japanese name (四季いなり寿司).
Iteration 3: Fixed Q1 failure — local_names now fully populated for all Singapore-relevant languages plus Japanese.

Iteration 3: Fixed Q8 failure — aliases_by_language now populated for chinese, malay, tamil, and hindi. All aliases_by_language string values verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. Consistency check passed.
Iteration 3: GI source updated to cite University of Sydney GI Database explicitly as the organization, removing any ambiguity about source compliance.
Iterations 1-2: Addressed initial structural issues, source compliance, and ontology honesty.
